skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render videos from React/Remotion component code via inference.sh. Pass TSX code, get MP4. Supports all Remotion APIs: useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence. Configurable resolution, FPS, duration, codec.
skill.summary: Render Remotion React components to MP4 videos using the inference.sh cloud rendering API.
skill.author: inference-sh-skills

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found 41 potential issues. After evaluation: all 3 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are false positives ('codec' refers to video codec, not cryptography). All 32 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are false positives (markdown fenced bash code blocks, not Ruby). All 6 'hardcoded URL' findings are false positives (documentation links to legitimate services). The skill is a legitimate Remotion video renderer that uses external commands (belt CLI) and network access (inference.sh API) as documented. No prompt injection or malicious intent detected. Published with medium risk and appropriate warnings.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution via Bash
medium_findings.description: The skill declares allowed-tools: Bash(belt *) granting broad shell execution. Multiple bash code examples invoke the belt CLI to send user-provided TSX code to a remote rendering API. While the usage is legitimate, unrestricted bash access poses risk if the AI tool executes commands with modified inputs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The bash commands are explicit documentation examples for a legitimate video rendering workflow. Risk is medium because the allowed-tools declaration is broad but the actual commands are well-scoped to the belt CLI tool.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs to External Services
low_findings.description: The skill contains hardcoded URLs to remotion.dev and inference.sh documentation. These are legitimate references for the skill's intended functionality but reveal dependence on external services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs point to official documentation pages for Remotion and inference.sh. No evidence of malicious domains or data exfiltration endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unrestricted Bash Permission Declaration
dangerous_patterns.description: Line 5 declares 'allowed-tools: Bash(belt *)' which grants the AI unrestricted bash execution via the belt tool. This broad permission could allow arbitrary command execution if the skill is used outside its intended workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is real but the tool scope is limited to the belt CLI. Risk depends on the sandboxing environment of the AI tool executing the skill.

content.user_title: Render Videos from React Code Using inference.sh
content.value_statement: Creating MP4 videos from React components requires complex rendering infrastructure that is difficult to set up locally. This skill sends your Remotion TSX code to the inference.sh cloud API and returns a ready-to-use MP4 video file with configurable resolution, FPS, and duration.
actual_capabilities: Render MP4 videos from Remotion React component code
Support all Remotion APIs including useCurrentFrame, spring, interpolate, and Sequence
Configure video width, height, FPS, duration, and output codec
Pass custom JSON props to video components for dynamic content
Stream render progress updates for long-running video generation
Integrate with Python applications using the inference.sh SDK
limitations: Requires internet access to connect to the inference.sh API
Not suitable for real-time video playback or live streaming
Dependent on external service availability and rate limits
Video quality is limited by the inference.sh rendering service capabilities

use_cases.title: Generate Marketing Videos Programmatically
use_cases.description: Create branded promotional videos by passing TSX components with logos, text, and animations to the rendering service.
use_cases.target_user: Marketing developers and content automation engineers

use_cases.title: Build Data-Driven Animation Pipelines
use_cases.description: Feed real-time data into Remotion components to generate dashboards, charts, and data visualizations as video content.

use_cases.title: Prototype Motion Designs Quickly
use_cases.description: Test animation concepts by writing React components and instantly rendering them to video without any local setup or configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Create a fade-in title animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a 3-second video with a title that fades in from transparent to fully visible over 1 second. Use the useCurrentFrame and interpolate functions from Remotion. Set resolution to 1920x1080 at 30 FPS.
prompt_templates.scenario: A beginner user wants to create a simple title card video.

prompt_templates.title: Generate an animated loading progress bar
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 5-second video showing an animated progress bar going from 0 to 100 percent. Use the spring animation function for smooth easing motion. Set width to 1080 and height to 1080 at 60 FPS.
prompt_templates.scenario: A developer needs a loading animation for an app prototype.

prompt_templates.title: Build a multi-scene video with sequences
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a 6-second video with three sequential scenes using the Sequence component. Each scene displays different text that fades in. First scene at frame 0, second at frame 60, third at frame 120. Use 30 FPS.
prompt_templates.scenario: A content creator needs an animated intro with multiple segments.

prompt_templates.title: Create a data-driven chart animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a 10-second video that visualizes quarterly sales data as animated bar charts. Pass the data values via component props. Use 60 FPS and 1920x1080 resolution. Each bar should animate sequentially.
prompt_templates.scenario: An analytics team member needs to convert quarterly reports into video summaries.

output_examples.input: Render a 3-second fade-in animation with black background and white text at 1920x1080.
output: Renders a 1920x1080 MP4 video file showing white text fading in from transparent to fully visible over 1 second against a black background.
Returns a download link to the rendered MP4 file hosted on inference.sh servers for direct download or embedding.

output_examples.input: Generate a 5-second animated loading counter from 0 to 100 percent at 1080x1080.
output: Renders a 1080x1080 MP4 video with a large percentage counter that counts from 0 to 100 percent over 5 seconds.
Displays a Loading subtitle text beneath the counter that remains visible throughout the animation.
Returns the video URL for download with smooth 60 FPS playback.

best_practices: Keep component code focused and avoid external dependencies beyond remotion and react for reliable rendering.
Set appropriate duration and FPS values to balance output quality with processing time on the remote service.
Use the Sequence component to organize multi-scene videos instead of complex conditional rendering logic.
anti_patterns: Do not include API keys, secrets, or sensitive data inside the TSX code passed to the renderer.
Avoid extremely long video durations as they may exceed API time limits and cause rendering failures.
Do not use browser-specific APIs like localStorage, document, or window in video components.

faq.question: What is Remotion?
faq.answer: Remotion is a library for creating videos programmatically using React components. It allows you to use web development skills to produce MP4 video content.

faq.question: Do I need to install Remotion locally?
faq.answer: No. The skill sends your Remotion code to the inference.sh API for remote rendering. You only need to provide the TSX component code as input.

faq.question: What video formats are supported?
faq.answer: The default output format is MP4. You can specify a different codec using the codec input parameter.

faq.question: Can I pass external data to my video?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the props input parameter to pass JSON data that your Remotion component can read and render in the video.

faq.question: How long does rendering take?
faq.answer: Render time depends on video length, resolution, and complexity. Simple short videos typically render in a few seconds.

faq.question: What Remotion APIs can I use?
faq.answer: All standard Remotion APIs including useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence, Audio, Video, and Img.
